title: Do You Need To Declutter Your Home?

blanks.sentence: Clutter fills homes in an untidy, disorganised way and may affect a person's ________.
blanks.evidence: "The clutter in my house is actually starting to affect my mental health. I can't think or work anymore..." (L15)

blanks.sentence: One listener woke at ________ because of the urge to tidy.
blanks.evidence: "I can't think or work anymore, so much so it woke me at 4 a.m. this morning and I felt a strong urge to tidy." (L15)

blanks.sentence: Georgie describes herself as a ________, someone with little stuff.
blanks.evidence: "I don't have a problem with clutter. I would describe myself as a minimalist. I don't have very much stuff." (L30)

blanks.sentence: A possible solution is ________, but decisions about what to keep can be hard.
blanks.evidence: "If you do, there's a simple solution: decluttering - or throwing away some of the unused stuff filling our homes. But deciding what to keep and what to throw away is not so easy..." (L36)

blanks.sentence: Michelle Ogundehin says ________ is more tiring than people realise.
blanks.evidence: "Visual clutter is more exhausting than we realise. It's not just stuff. Our brains are constantly processing our surroundings..." (L52)

blanks.sentence: Our brains may have to ________ when surrounded by chaos.
blanks.evidence: "Our brains are constantly processing our surroundings, so they have to work overtime if they're surrounded by chaos." (L52)

blanks.sentence: A cluttered home can leave people feeling ________.
blanks.evidence: "So, if you feel drained or exhausted at home, it could be your clutter... when you clear away the stuff that has no purpose in your life, you will feel better..." (L52)

blanks.sentence: Sorting possessions can be ________ because objects may carry feelings.
blanks.evidence: "One reason people put off decluttering is the time it takes. Plus, it can be emotionally disturbing deciding what to keep and what to throw away." (L61)

blanks.sentence: Ingrid recommends clearing ________ at a time.
blanks.evidence: "You have to do decluttering slowly but surely: one drawer, one cupboard at a time. You can't go in all guns blazing." (L64)

blanks.sentence: Trying to do too much may mean the task becomes ________ for you.
blanks.evidence: "They've bitten off [too much that] more than they can chew... if you bite off more than you can chew, you try to do something which is too difficult for you." (L64-L70)

multipleChoice.question: What is the main focus of the discussion?
options: Why people buy too many unnecessary household items.
How clutter affects people and how to manage it gradually.
Why minimalist homes are becoming popular on social media.
How television programmes have changed interior design habits.
multipleChoice.evidence: "The clutter in my house is actually starting to affect my mental health... Visual clutter is more exhausting than we realise... You have to do decluttering slowly but surely..." (L15-L64)

multipleChoice.question: What problem does the listener's message describe?
options: Clutter has become so stressful that it affects thinking and work.
Clutter has forced the listener to move house within a week.
Clutter has made the listener spend too much money on storage.
Clutter has damaged the listener's relationships with children.
multipleChoice.evidence: "The clutter in my house is actually starting to affect my mental health. I can't think or work anymore... I felt a strong urge to tidy." (L15)

multipleChoice.question: What attitude does Michelle Ogundehin express towards clutter?
options: She thinks people exaggerate its emotional impact.
She sees it as tiring because the brain must process it.
She believes it is mainly a problem of bad decoration.
She treats it as harmless if the home is still functional.
multipleChoice.evidence: "Visual clutter is more exhausting than we realise. It's not just stuff. Our brains are constantly processing our surroundings, so they have to work overtime..." (L52)

multipleChoice.question: Why can visual clutter make people feel exhausted?
options: It makes people work longer hours at home.
It encourages people to buy more possessions.
It forces the brain to keep processing disorder.
It prevents people from remembering where things are.
multipleChoice.evidence: "Our brains are constantly processing our surroundings, so they have to work overtime if they're surrounded by chaos. So, if you feel drained or exhausted at home, it could be your clutter." (L52)

multipleChoice.question: What contrast is made in the advice about decluttering?
options: Clearing drawers is useful, while clearing cupboards is harmful.
Social media makes decluttering easier, while television makes it harder.
Quick dramatic action may create more chaos; gradual work is safer.
Physical tiredness matters more than emotional difficulty.
multipleChoice.evidence: "You have to do decluttering slowly but surely: one drawer, one cupboard at a time. You can't go in all guns blazing... 'There's more chaos than there was before!'" (L64)

multipleChoice.question: What is Ingrid Jansen's view on starting a decluttering task?
options: People should first empty the whole room to see all their possessions.
People should begin with small areas and proceed steadily.
People should wait until they have a full weekend free.
People should focus only on items with no financial value.
multipleChoice.evidence: "You have to do decluttering slowly but surely: one drawer, one cupboard at a time. You can't go in all guns blazing." (L64)

multipleChoice.question: In "they've bitten off more than they can chew", what does the phrase refer to?
options: Starting a decluttering job that is too big or difficult.
Keeping too many old clothes and books in one room.
Feeling guilty about throwing away useful objects.
Copying advice from a television design programme.
multipleChoice.evidence: "This weekend, I'm going to sort out my whole room of doom... There's more chaos than there was before! because they've bitten off [too much that] more than they can chew." (L64)

multipleChoice.question: What can be inferred about why people delay decluttering?
options: They may avoid it because it requires time and emotional decisions.
They usually do not believe clutter affects mental health.
They think decluttering only works if done dramatically.
They mainly fear that other people will judge their homes.
multipleChoice.evidence: "One reason people put off decluttering is the time it takes. Plus, it can be emotionally disturbing deciding what to keep and what to throw away." (L61)

multipleChoice.question: Why is the "room of doom" example mentioned?
options: To show that cluttered rooms are usually impossible to improve.
To illustrate how overambitious plans can quickly become chaotic.
To recommend clearing the worst room before any small spaces.
To prove that most people stop decluttering after two hours.
multipleChoice.evidence: "'This weekend, I'm going to sort out my whole room of doom.' And after two hours in... 'What have I done? There's more chaos than there was before!'" (L64)

multipleChoice.question: What is the broader conclusion of the discussion?
options: Decluttering is valuable, but it should be approached in manageable steps.
A clutter-free home is possible only for people who are minimalists.
Social media has made people more anxious about home organisation.
The main purpose of decluttering is to improve interior design.
multipleChoice.evidence: "When it comes to decluttering your home, it's best to do things slowly but surely... So, if you want to declutter without stress, remember: break the job down, and go slow." (L67-L73)
